schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Elijah's translation recompensed his services, proved another life in a degenerate age, and typified Christ's ascension.
Wesley and Clarke: Elijah went body and soul, changed to fit the world of spirits; the event was foreknown to prophet, disciple, and the schools.
Ellicott dates it to Jehoram's reign, reads 'into heaven' as an accusative of direction, and notes the LXX makes God, not the visible sky, the goal.
Gill: Elijah's movements were made 'under a divine direction,' God having given him notice of his end.
Ellicott: Elijah's dismissals were neither test nor humility but genuine uncertainty whether God willed Elisha to come; the errand was to steady the prophetic schools.
Clarke and Wesley: humility or the trying of Elisha's love; 'to Bethel' was truth, though not the whole truth of Elijah's journey.
Gill lists the classic motives — modesty, sparing Elisha grief, or testing his knowledge and love.

debates.question: Why does Elijah repeatedly tell Elisha to stay behind?

sides.position: humility, or a test of Elisha's affection and fidelity
genuine uncertainty whether God willed Elisha to accompany him

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the schools were divinely forewarned to strengthen their faith; 'from thy head' means self, or the master's position of superiority.
Wesley: the revelation passed from a few to the whole college; 'hold your peace' is grief asking not to be aggravated.
Gill: Bethel, though a seat of Jeroboam's calf, held a prophetic school planted as a check on the idolatry of the times.

debates.question: What does 'take away thy master from thy head' mean?

sides.position: 'head' means self or person, or the master's position of authority over the disciple
a literal reference to Elijah's being taken up 'over thy head'

schools.summary: Ellicott sees in the exact repetition a trace of oral tradition preserved in the prophetic guilds; Keil sees a deliberate farewell visitation with a higher purpose.
Gill anchors the geography, Elijah bidding Elisha tarry at Bethel as the journey advances toward Jericho.

debates.question: Why does the narrative repeat itself so exactly?

sides.position: a mark of oral transmission within the prophetic guilds
a deliberate, purposeful farewell circuit of the schools

schools.summary: Gill: Jericho, rebuilt under Joshua's curse, was nonetheless 'blessed with a school of the prophets' — grace planted where judgment had fallen.
Keil gathers the converging approach to Jordan; Ellicott refines 'came' to 'drew near' and 'answered' to 'said.'
Ellicott (with Reuss): the monotony of the repeated scenes deepens the solemnity; all the disciples share the foreboding, and Elisha clings as if to hold his master back.
Gill: Elijah seems eager to be rid of Elisha lest he witness the assumption, echoing the debated motive of v.2.
Clarke and Wesley: the fifty knew by revelation and came not from curiosity but to be appointed witnesses of the translation to others.
Ellicott: they stood directly opposite, at a distance, curious to see how the two would cross a river where there was no ford.
Gill: they stood afar off to view the assumption and be witnesses of it.
Ellicott: a symbolic action like Moses' rod, where the outward sign becomes the channel of faith; the mantle is the hairy addereth marking the prophet.
Clarke calls it an astonishing miracle by God's power alone; Wesley makes the parted Jordan a figure of death made passable by Christ's death.
Gill investigates the mantle, weighing whether it was the hairy garment or a goat's-skin melote worn by prophets.
Keil and Ellicott: not twice Elijah's own spirit but the firstborn's double share among the sons of the prophets, per the law of Deut. 21:17.
Clarke grounds it in the firstborn's portion; Wesley notes God gave Elisha even more than he asked, perhaps beyond Elijah's own measure.
Matthew Henry: a holy ambition that asks not for wealth but to be qualified for God's service, and asks it as God's gift to be interceded for.
Gill: a rare privilege, difficult to obtain, granted to few.

debates.question: What is the 'double portion' Elisha requests?

sides.position: the firstborn's double share among the sons of the prophets — to be Elijah's true heir
twice the measure of Elijah's own spirit (rejected by the commentators)

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'hard' because beyond Elijah's power, tied wholly to God's will; the sign implies a departure above ordinary perception.
Clarke: the gift cannot hang on mere seeing but on perseverance to the end; Wesley: the sign was set to make Elisha wait and pray more fervently.
Matthew Henry: calling it hard teaches Elisha to prize it; close attendance to the last is the means of obtaining the spirit.
Gill: a rare blessing few are favoured with.

debates.question: What does the condition 'if thou see me when I am taken' require?

sides.position: faithful perseverance to the end, of which the seeing is the shorthand and seal
a God-appointed sign to spur Elisha to wait and pray, marking his worthiness

schools.summary: Clarke: Elijah was truly translated into heaven, against all rationalizing; Wesley: the fire and horses were angels come to bear him up in triumph.
Ellicott: the storm-language must not be over-literalized — God took Elijah amid a display of natural power; Keil holds a real fiery phenomenon seen by Elisha.

debates.question: How literally should the fiery chariot and horses be taken?

sides.position: a real, visible fiery convoy of angels bearing Elijah bodily to heaven
the storm-imagery of God's power; the popular fiery-car picture is read into, not from, the text

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: 'chariot and horsemen of Israel' names Elijah as the nation's truest defence, worth more than its armies (cf. the Targum).
Wesley: 'my father' voices affection and authority; Clarke relays the Chaldee — Elijah by intercession was more use to Israel than horses and chariots.
Matthew Henry: Elisha felt himself a fatherless child thrown upon the world, and mourned accordingly.
Gill: the ascension answers to Christ's, and the seeing is Elisha's token of the promised double portion.
Matthew Henry: the mantle fell as Elijah was 'clothed upon with immortality'; the remains of good men — sayings, writings, examples — are to be gathered and worn, not worshipped.
Keil and Ellicott: the fallen mantle is the pledge and public sign that Elisha is the divinely appointed successor on whom Elijah's spirit rests.
Clarke and Wesley: possessing the mantle proved Elisha invested with his master's authority and a pledge that the Spirit would rest on him.
Gill: the mantle falling to Elisha tokened his succession and the promised double portion.
Matthew Henry: 'Where is the LORD God of Elijah?' is inquiry after God, not the departed prophet; those who follow the fathers' steps meet the fathers' grace.
Ellicott: an 'irony of faith' seeking Jehovah as sole power; he and Keil untangle the difficult aph hu, 'yea He,' and the versions' variants.
Clarke reports the Vulgate's reading of a first, unavailing stroke followed by a second after invoking Elijah's God, unsupported by other versions.
Gill: Elisha smote the waters to try whether the same spirit and power now rested on him.

outliers: A stray Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment about a Philistine commander is mis-filed here and irrelevant to the verse.
themes: Where is the God of Elijah
the unchanging God of the prophets

schools.summary: Matthew Henry and Wesley: schooled men bowed to the ploughman Elisha once they saw God with him — evident divine presence outweighs rank and education.
Ellicott and Keil: the repeated miracle proved Elijah's spirit had 'alighted' on Elisha; likely the whole Jericho guild is meant.
Clarke: seeing the mantle and the same miracle, they naturally yielded him the obedience once given his master.
Gill: those who had gone toward Jordan to glimpse the assumption saw Elisha cross the parted waters and recognized him.
Ellicott: they expected to find Elijah alive, not to bury him; their very search proves the ascension was not the popular fiery-car spectacle.
Wesley and Clarke: they thought Elijah taken only for a time, or his body cast down for honourable burial; Elisha refused, knowing he was in heaven.
Gill: the fifty were young and strong, fit to travel many days on the search.

debates.question: What did the sons of the prophets expect the search to find?

sides.position: Elijah still alive, set down by the Spirit on some mountain or valley
his body, cast down, to give it honourable burial

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: wise men may yield for peace to what their judgment opposes; the vain search would make the disciples defer to Elisha next time.
Ellicott parses 'unto being ashamed' — embarrassed, disconcerted — surveying Thenius, Keil, and Bähr; Keil carries on the three-day search.
Clarke and Wesley: Elisha yielded to relentless importunity lest a refusal seem contempt of his master or of them.
Gill: he consented, unable to deny their pressing and importunate request any longer.
Ellicott: 'Go not' is imperative — Elisha now presses his vindicated authority; with this the history of Elijah closes, his spirit passing to his disciples (Reuss).
Matthew Henry: traversing hills and valleys never brings us to Elijah, but the imitation of his faith and zeal will, in due time.
Gill: Elisha tarried at Jericho awaiting the searchers' report.
Matthew Henry: Jericho, built in defiance of a curse, becomes a nursery of piety; prophets love to be employed, and common mercies like water should be prized.
Ellicott: 'the city of palms' had a fine site; the fault was the bad water, which caused dead births — v.21 shows the spring, not the soil, was the evil.
Clarke: the barren ground was the effect of the bad water; Wesley: whether original or from Hiel's curse, the land grew fruitful after the cure.
Ellicott: the new vessel had to be uncontaminated by use, and the salt is an appropriate sacramental medium expelling the spring's corruption.
Wesley: salt is the wrong remedy — it makes water brackish — proving the cure was by divine power, working without means or against them.
Gill: a never-used cruse, so none could think the virtue lay in anything previously in it.
Matthew Henry: the sufferers must fetch the means and have faith tried; God's grace works in observance of His institutions, not our operations.
Clarke: striking the source, not the stream, and the cure's permanence prove a true miracle, as even Jarchi allowed of corrupting salt.
Ellicott: the Divine creative will, not the salt, healed the fountain; the waters would no longer bring death or 'make abortion.'
Matthew Henry: 'Thus saith the LORD' makes Elisha only the channel; to renew a life, renew the heart at the source with the salt of grace.
Wesley: 'death' means the hurt or danger the bad water brought to man or beast.

themes: salt of grace and the heart

schools.summary: Gill: the healing endured to Josephus's day and to ours, confirmed by Maundrell, Pliny's 'Calirroe,' and travellers who found the spring still sweet.
Ellicott: 'unto this day' marks when the narrative was first written; the endurance shows the cure altered the water's very nature.
Matthew Henry: what God does is for ever — when His Spirit heals a soul the property is altered, no more death or barrenness.
Clarke and Wesley: the mockers were young men old enough to know good from evil; 'Go up' blasphemed the ascension and God Himself.
Ellicott: young boys of various ages; doubts they knew of the ascension, taking 'go up' of the road Elisha climbed, baldness a reproach and a mark of leprosy.
Matthew Henry: they aimed not at his baldness but his prophetic office, taught by idolatrous parents — a crying sin of Israel.
Gill: Elisha came to Bethel to report Elijah's assumption and confirm his authority among the sons of the prophets.

debates.question: Who were the mockers, and how grave was the taunt?

sides.position: young men, accountable, blaspheming the ascension and God
boys of mixed ages who likely knew nothing of the ascension; 'go up' meant the road

schools.summary: Wesley: no revenge but the motion of God's Spirit; the mockery was malignity against God and His work, and idolatrous Bethel deserved death by the law.
Keil and Ellicott: the immorality objection falls on God who executed the curse; the mobbing may have been premeditated, the judgment fitting an age of judgment (Lev. 26:21).
Matthew Henry: the prophet is justified by divine impulse, God said Amen; God is glorified as righteous who reckons sin even in children.
Gill: the stern look was a warning to shame them, ignored as they repeated their mockery.

debates.question: How can the destruction of the mockers be justified?

sides.position: it was God's own judicial act through His prophet on a taught contempt for God, not Elisha's revenge

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Elisha withdrew to Carmel's caves for solitude, prayer, and the memory of his master, then returned to his permanent house in Samaria.
Matthew Henry (with Bishop Hall): Carmel for contemplation, Samaria for service — none is a profitable seer who is either always or never alone.
Gill: Carmel was a place Elijah frequented, perhaps holding a school of the prophets.
